Bibliography-and-Acquisition-Guide.title: Bibliography and Acquisition Guide
Bibliography-and-Acquisition-Guide.content:
Bibliography and Acquisition Guide
This guide consolidates source targets from internal analysis and external findings, with practical acquisition pathways.
Books
1) Lewis County, West Virginia: A Pictorial History of Old Lewis County, the Crossroads of Central West Virginia (Gilchrist & Gilchrist)

Amazon: www.amazon.com/Lewis-County-West-Virginia-crossroads/dp/0898658675
AbeBooks: www.abebooks.com/Lewis-County-West-Virginia-pictorial-history/32167392339/bd
eBay listing context: www.ebay.com/p/5604607
Use case: local settlement patterns, institutions, contextual corroboration.

2) The Oil Well Driller (Charles A. Whiteshot, 1905)

Internet Archive text: archive.org/stream/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich_djvu.txt
Acquisition note: free digital access; consider print-on-demand copy for annotation.
Use case: Copley No. 1 Oil Strike historical and technical context.

3) Additional named works from strategy narrative

The Book of Surnames (Peter Verstappen) — search major used-book platforms.
Lonely Planet Ireland references (Smallman/Yale/Fallon) — contextual geography only; low genealogical priority.


Articles, Databases, and Online References
Irish research portals

National Library of Ireland parish registers: registers.nli.ie
Roscommon tithe books index: titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie/pagestab/Roscommon/
Ask About Ireland (Griffith’s Valuation): www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/
Leitrim-Roscommon Griffith PDF access: leitrim-roscommon.com/GRIFFITH/Griffiths.PDF

U.S. records and military

NARA immigration records: www.archives.gov/research/immigration
NARA Civil War military records: www.archives.gov/research/military/civil-war
Military service request information: www.archives.gov/veterans/military-service-records

West Virginia and local history

Lewis County genealogy guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy
Historical marker context for Copley No. 1: www.hmdb.org/results.asp%20Virginia&County=Lewis%20County
e-WV entries:

Irish in WV: www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/830
Lewis County: www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/1313



Australia/transportation research

Trove: trove.nla.gov.au
Convict Records: convictrecords.com.au


Archives and Repositories (with Acquisition Strategy)
A) Lewis County Courthouse (Weston, WV)

Priority records:

Deed books (land/title chain and oil lease entries, c.1840–1905)
Probate records (especially estate of John Copley, d. 1925)
Marriage registers (for Q20)


Request strategy:

Call clerk to confirm index format and copy fees.
Request book/page lookup using name variants (Copley/Copely; Dolan).
Order certified and non-certified scans (both if available).


Estimated cost: 1–3/page copies, plus certification/postage.

B) West Virginia State Archives (Charleston, WV)

Priority records:

State-level vital records and county abstracts
Historical newspapers for death/marriage notices
Land and military abstracts


Request strategy:

Search catalog first; then submit targeted pull requests.
Use exact date windows derived from census/manifests.
Ask staff for adjacent-reel suggestions when no-hit occurs.


Estimated cost: travel + copy/scanning fees.

C) National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)

Priority records:

Civil War service and pension packets (Q28)
Quartermaster Department references (RG 92)
Naturalization and immigration holdings (Q19)


Request strategy:

File formal request with full identity matrix (name variants, residence, age).
Request negative-search statement if no file found.
Correlate findings with local county records.


Estimated cost: depends on reproduction level; plan 30–150+ per file set.

D) Irish repositories (NLI + National Archives of Ireland)

Priority records:

Kilgefin parish baptisms/marriages (Q1, Q3)
Tithe and valuation records for surname variants


Request strategy:

Build surname-variant sheet (Copley/Copely/Copple/etc.).
Review by townland/parish, not by surname-only search.
Capture images and full citation paths for each candidate entry.


Estimated cost: mostly online/free; optional paid researcher if on-site needed.

E) Diocesan/Catholic parish archives (MD/VA/WV corridor)

Priority records:

Sacramental marriage entries for Michael Copley + Ann Elizabeth Munday Copley and John Copley + Mary Ellen Dolan Copley.


Request strategy:

Contact diocesan archivist with narrow date windows.
Include witness name possibilities and settlement clues.
Ask whether records are centralized or parish-retained.


Estimated cost: donation + search/copy fees.


Hard-to-Find Sources: Practical Acquisition Playbook


Out-of-print county books

Track on AbeBooks, eBay, Biblio, and WorldCat alerts.
Budget: 35–250 depending on condition/rarity.



Local CD/photo archives mentioned in family documents

Contact local historical societies in Lewis County.
Ask for inventory sheets and digitization availability.
Budget: variable media transfer fees.



Microfilm-only records

Use FamilySearch affiliate libraries and major genealogy centers.
Pre-order reels/collections where possible.
Budget: travel + printing/scanning.



Newspapers behind paywalls

Compare subscription cost vs. one-time lookup request.
Consider local library remote access credentials.




Suggested Contact Targets

Lewis County Clerk (WV) — deeds, probate, marriage books.
West Virginia State Archives — statewide record support and newspaper access.
NARA reference staff — federal military/naturalization holdings.
NLI / Irish genealogy services — parish-register retrieval guidance.
Marshall/Lewis County historical societies — local Dolan/Copley context.

(Use official websites for current phone/email; contact details change frequently.)

Cross-links

Research questions: Phase 1 Questions and Answers
People targets: People Directory
Contextual themes: Topics and Themes
Narrative synthesis: The Copley Family Narrative

CLAUDE.title: CLAUDE
CLAUDE.content:
CLAUDE.md — Copley Family Research Vault
This file gives AI agents instant orientation for working on this project. Read it before doing anything else.
Project Overview
A genealogical research wiki for the Copley family, spanning Irish immigrant ancestors (c.1813) through G28 great-great-grandchildren. Maintained by Zach Copley (zcopley@gmail.com), who is a G26 descendant in the Tom Copley line.

Live site: zcopley.github.io/copley-family-research/
Repo: github.com/zcopley/copley-family-research
Local clone: /mnt/c/Users/zach/Projects/copley-family-research
Stack: Quartz v4 (static site generator), Obsidian-flavored Markdown, GitHub Actions CI/CD
Deployment: Every push to main auto-builds (Node 22) and deploys to gh-pages via .github/workflows/deploy.yml


Repository Structure
/                          ← vault root (all .md files here are published)
├── People/                ← individual person profiles
├── Places/                ← place pages (Lewis County, Baltimore, Ireland, etc.)
├── Topics/                ← thematic event/research pages
├── References/            ← source documents and social media profiles
├── quartz/                ← Quartz framework (do not edit)
├── Family Tree.md         ← Mermaid diagram, all generations

Key Facts About the User (Zach Copley)

Zach is a G26 descendant — Tom Copley’s son
He is the primary source for living family (G26–G28) biographical details
He may not know much about the Stephen line cousins
He may have family documents to upload for the Stephen line
Always ask Zach to verify facts about living family before publishing

8 children: Michael (b.1959), Sara, Philip, Paul, Peter, Susan, Stephen J. (d.2023), Amy (b.1990)
Most G26 Stephen line pages are stubs — priority for future work


Build & Deploy
# CI/CD handles this automatically on every push to main
# To build locally (requires Node 22):
npm ci
npx quartz build
 
# Manual deploy (if needed):
npx quartz sync
The GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) runs on every push to main, builds with Node 22, and pushes public/ to gh-pages. The local system has Node 10 — do not attempt local builds.

What Not To Do

Do not edit files in quartz/ — that’s the framework
Do not commit .abacus.donotdelete — it’s environment metadata
Do not attempt npm run build locally — Node version is too old (system has v10, need v22)
Do not add unsourced biographical facts about living people without flagging them as unverified
Do not use “Brian Bird” — it’s Bryan Bird
Do not conflate Thomas E. Copley (1892–1968, G24) with Thomas Partlow Copley (b.1944, G25)
Do not conflate Michael Copley Sr. (1813–1897) with Michael Joseph Copley (1898–1988) with Michael Copley (b.1959)

Home.title: Home
Home.content:
Copley Family Research Vault
Welcome to the Copley family Obsidian vault. This vault consolidates evidence, hypotheses, open questions, and narrative history for the family line from Michael Copley (b. 1813) through modern descendants.
Project Overview
This project is based on:

Internal analysis: /home/ubuntu/copley_research_analysis.md
External findings: /home/ubuntu/copley_research_findings.md
Source PDFs:

Copley_Research_Strategy.pdf
P1APPENDICES.pdf
COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf



The research focus includes:

Irish origins in Roscommon/Fermanagh
Immigration to the United States (1830s)
Settlement and land acquisition in Lewis County, WV
Industrial turning point: the 1900 1900 Copley Oil Strike
Intergenerational mobility from farming to professional careers

Vault Navigation
Core Pages

Family Tree ⭐
Phase 1 Questions and Answers
People Directory
People Directory (Individual Profiles)
Topics and Themes
Topics Index
Places Index
The Copley Family Narrative
Bibliography and Acquisition Guide

Recommended Reading Path

Start with Family Tree for a visual map of family relationships and generations.
Continue to Phase 1 Questions and Answers for active research priorities.
Use People Directory to orient people and generations.
Read Topics and Themes for historical context.
Open Topics Index for detailed event/theme pages.
Open Places Index for location-by-location research planning.
Enjoy The Copley Family Narrative for a synthesized story.
Use Bibliography and Acquisition Guide to acquire records/books and plan archival work.

Research Methodology
This vault uses an evidence-tier framework:

Primary evidence: manifests, deeds, census, parish records, military files.
Secondary synthesis: county histories, compiled genealogies, historical essays.
Oral history: family tradition requiring corroboration.
Speculative hypotheses: clearly marked with italics and bracketed notes.

All uncertain claims are labeled and paired with targeted acquisition strategies (archive, repository, or database actions).

People-Directory.content:
People Directory

Generational notation follows the project convention from G23 to G28 as used in the strategy and appendices.


Phase 1A profile pages are now available in People Directory and the People/ folder.


📊 Start with Family Tree for the fastest visual orientation across generations and branches.

Name Disambiguation Notes

Michael Copley: use Michael Copley (1813–1897), Michael Joseph Copley (1898–1988), or Michael Copley (b. 1959).
Anne Copley: use Anne Copley (b. 1850) or Anne Copley (daughter of John).
Ann/Anne distinction: Ann Copley is the matriarch (1823–1909); both Anne pages are daughters in later generations.

G23 — Foundational Ancestors and Siblings
Michael Copley (1813–1897)

Born: Kilgefin, Ireland (per gravestone tradition).
Died: Lewis County, West Virginia.
Roles: immigrant laborer/farmer; probable B&O Railroad Labor phase before settlement.
Key event: 1843 land agreement with brother Patrick.
Research gaps: Q1 (parentage), Q3 (marriage), Q19 (naturalization).

Ann Copley (1823–1909)

Born: Kinawley, Ireland (County Fermanagh context; reported in family narrative).
Family tradition: father drowned in Potomac.
Maiden surname recorded as “Munday” in secondary family sources, still requiring primary-record verification.
Research gaps: Q3, Q11/Q14 (marriage documentation, father identity, wider Munday reconstruction).

Patrick Copley

Michael’s brother; co-purchaser in 1843 land agreement.
Died 1865.
Gap: stronger documentary profile beyond land references.

William Copley

Brother linked by oral history to anti-tithe unrest and possible Australian transport.
Gap: Q6 documentary confirmation pending.

Bridget Copley Reynolds

Sister, born c.1808; reportedly immigrant.
Gap: U.S. residence and descendants need systematic reconstruction.

Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon

Sister, born c.1811.
Gap: Q8/Q10 unresolved immigration details and surname variants.


G24 — Lewis County Continuity
Immediate children of Michael Copley + Ann Copley (new Phase 1A pages)

Mary Copley Quinn
John Copley
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley (b. 1850)
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley

Sarah Copley

John Copley (1843–1925)

Son of Michael/Ann; inherited family farm.
Married Mary Ellen Dolan Copley.
Research gaps: Q20 (marriage record), Q28 (military claim), Q21 (late marriage context).

Mary Ellen Dolan Copley (1855–1901)

From Dolan family (Moundsville connection in current narrative).
Mother of five surviving children.
Research gaps: Q20 record confirmation, Q22 sibling reconstruction, Q31 death detail expansion.


G24 — Children of John + Mary Ellen (branch-parent generation for Phase 1B)
Thomas E. Copley

Born 1892; died 1968 (reported).
Son of John Copley and Mary Ellen Dolan Copley; married Drusilla.
No children are documented in current family records.

Mary Copley Flesch

Daughter; later associated with Albuquerque.
Gap: fuller household/career timeline.

Anne Copley (daughter of John)

Daughter; reportedly unmarried; relocated to Albuquerque.
Gap: census and city-directory continuity.

Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo (1897–1977)

Registered nurse and educator; key Maryland branch matriarch.
Parent of Phase 1B G25 descendants: Sarah Ellen Sardo Arena, Mary Carmella Sardo Ruland.

Michael Joseph Copley (1898–1988)

Research chemist; BS Catholic University; PhD University of Illinois; USDA leadership.
Parent of Phase 1B G25 descendants: Stephen Michael Copley, Thomas Partlow Copley.


Phase 1B G25 — Children of John Copley’s children (new comprehensive pages)
Sarah Ellen Sardo Arena

Child of Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo and Robert Samuel Sardo.
Arena branch connector; profile expanded with evidence gaps/strategies.

Mary Carmella Sardo Ruland

Child of Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo and Robert Samuel Sardo.
Ruland branch matriarch; profile expanded with evidence gaps/strategies.

Stephen Michael Copley

Child of Michael Joseph Copley and Marion Elizabeth Partlow.
Physicist/professor/administrator; expanded professional biography and source map.

Thomas Partlow Copley

Child of Michael Joseph Copley and Marion Elizabeth Partlow.
Marketing/academia profile expanded from Appendix 1 biographical material.

Tentative / possible additions (explicitly unverified)

Possible additional G25 descendants through Mary Copley Flesch or Anne Copley (daughter of John) remain unconfirmed in currently available records.

People/Amy-E.-Copley-Geist.content:
Amy Elizabeth Copley Geist (b. July 11, 1990)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Amy Elizabeth Copley Geist is a G26 descendant in the Stephen Michael Copley line, the only child of Stephen and his second wife Judith Ann Todd Copley.

Full name: Amy Elizabeth Copley Geist
Birth: July 11, 1990, Hinsdale, Illinois (suburb of Chicago)
Parents: Stephen Michael Copley and Judith Ann Todd Copley
Spouse: Nathan (“Nate”) Geist — married June 19, 2021, ReFarm Café, State College, PA

People/Ann-Copley.title: Ann Copley
People/Ann-Copley.content:
Ann Copley (1823–1909)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Ann Copley is the matriarch of the West Virginia Copley line. Family narratives identify her as Ann Elizabeth Munday, born in 1823 in Kinawley, Ireland (County Fermanagh context), with immigration to the United States in childhood. However, because the primary marriage and birth records have not yet been located, her maiden surname remains a high-priority documentary verification gap.
She married Michael Copley (date/place unknown) and lived in Lewis County after the family’s 1843 land settlement. The 1850 census framework and family reconstruction identify her as mother of eight known children.
A persistent family tradition states that her father drowned in the Potomac River. This is plausible in context but currently unverified by contemporary records.
Key Place Links

Birthplace context: Kinawley, Ireland
Settlement context: Lewis County, West Virginia and Weston, West Virginia
Migration-corridor research hub: Baltimore, Maryland

Related Topic Pages

Irish Famine and Emigration
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
B&O Railroad Labor History

Family Relationships

Husband: Michael Copley
Children (G24):

Mary Copley Quinn
John Copley
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley
Thomas “Tom” Copley
Sarah Copley



Known/Reported Life Data

Born: 1823, Kinawley parish, Fermanagh (reported in family narrative)
Died: 1909 (family narrative)
Marriage: to Michael Copley, likely c.1838-1839 (inferred from first child birth year)

Research Gaps

Maiden-name proof: “Munday” is widely used in family material but requires direct primary-record confirmation.
Marriage record (Q3): No civil/church record yet found for Michael + Ann.
Immigration details: Port/date/manifest for Ann not yet resolved.
Father identity + drowning event (Q11/Q14): No coroner or newspaper confirmation yet found.
Wider Munday household reconstruction (Q14/Q15): Parents/siblings remain incomplete.

Acquisition Strategy

Search Catholic parish marriage registers in Potomac/B&O labor corridor communities (late 1830s to early 1840s).
Survey 1840-1850 census clusters near B&O settlements for Munday surname variants.
Use Chronicling America and regional newspaper repositories for Potomac drowning incidents matching family tradition.
Expand to PRONI and Irish/Fermanagh parish records for potential Ann Munday baptismal candidates.
Reconcile maiden-name evidence across gravestone, death certificates of children, and church sacramental records.

Source Citations

COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf (sections on Ann’s origins, marriage uncertainty, Potomac tradition, and children).
/home/ubuntu/copley_research_analysis.md (Q3, Q11, Q14, Q15 framing).

People/John-Copley.title: John Copley
People/John-Copley.content:
John Copley (1843–1925)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
John Copley was born 3 Nov 1843 near Weston, West Virginia (then Virginia), the son of Michael Copley and Ann Copley. He became the principal successor to the family farm established through the 1843 Hoffman agreement in the Copley Road area.
He married Mary Ellen Dolan Copley (date/place unresolved) and the couple had five children: Thomas E. Copley, Mary Copley Flesch, Anne Copley, Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo, and Michael Joseph Copley.
John’s household sat at the center of the family’s economic transformation following the 1900 1900 Copley Oil Strike. Probate/lease details remain to be fully documented, but his generation is the key bridge between immigrant agrarian roots and later professional mobility of descendants.

People/Marcia-Thornton-Copley.title: Marcia Thornton Copley
People/Marcia-Thornton-Copley.content:
Marcia Thornton Copley (first wife of Stephen Michael Copley)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Marcia Thornton Copley was the first wife of Stephen Michael Copley, whom she married while both were students at UC Berkeley. She bore seven children and managed the household largely alone as Stephen pursued his research career. After the challenges of raising a severely disabled child strained the marriage, she and Stephen divorced in 1983. She later earned a college degree, launched a career in developmental disabilities services, and remarried.

First marriage: Stephen Michael Copley (married at UC Berkeley; divorced 1983)
Second marriage: Edward (Ed) Dawson — founder of Social Vocational Services (SVS), a non-profit serving the developmentally disabled
Education: BA in Human Services, Cal State Dominguez Hills (completed after divorce; education had been interrupted when she married Stephen)
Career: Client Service Coordinator, Harbor Regional Center — notably the first parent of a client to apply for and be employed by the Harbor Regional Center; dedicated her career to helping the developmentally disabled and their families

Family Relationships

First spouse: Stephen Michael Copley (divorced 1983)
Second spouse: Ed Dawson (founder of SVS)
Children with Stephen (G26):

Michael Copley (b. 1959)
Sara Copley Cox (b. Feb 3, 1961)
Philip Copley (b. 1962)
Paul Copley (b. 1964)
Peter Copley (b. Jan 12, 1966)
Susan Copley (b. 1967)
Stephen Joseph Copley (b. May 1, 1970; d. Jun 10, 2023)



Notes

Her son Stephen Joseph Copley was diagnosed as autistic and severely developmentally disabled; his care profoundly shaped the family and directly influenced Marcia’s career path.
SVS (Social Vocational Services), founded by her second husband Ed Dawson in 1973, grew to serve thousands of individuals at 80+ locations throughout California. Several of Stephen’s children also worked for SVS at various points.
Marcia is a descendant (through her mother’s side) of Patrick Breen, a survivor of the ill-fated Donner Party. Patrick established a ranch near Mission San Juan Bautista — where Stephen Joseph was buried in 2023.

Sources

~/Downloads/Part 1 Appendices .pdf — Stephen Michael Copley and Stephen Joseph Copley biographical sketches; Sara Cox biographical sketch.

People/Marion-Elizabeth-Partlow.title: Marion Elizabeth Partlow
People/Marion-Elizabeth-Partlow.content:
Marion Elizabeth Partlow (1905–1977)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Marion Elizabeth Partlow was the wife of Michael Joseph Copley and mother of Stephen Michael Copley and Thomas Partlow Copley. Before marriage she had been an elementary school teacher. She was known as a devoted homemaker, excellent hostess (during Michael’s years as USDA lab director), and enthusiastic gardener who wrote a weekly wildflower column for a local California newspaper.

Birth: ~1905, Casey, Illinois (midway between Indianapolis and St. Louis)
Death: 1977 (age approximately 72)
Marriage: Michael Joseph Copley, 1933
Burial: Sunset View Cemetery, El Cerrito, California (with Michael)
Occupation (pre-marriage): Elementary school teacher
Residence: Champaign IL → Wyndmoor PA → Berkeley / El Cerrito CA

Family of Origin
The Partlow family immigrated from Wales to Virginia before the Revolutionary War, then moved west to Indiana, then Illinois. They owned a large farm near Casey, Illinois.

Brothers: Harry Partlow, Raymond Partlow, Lester (“Leck”) Partlow
Sisters: Laura Partlow, Doris Partlow, Genevieve (“Gen”) Partlow

Family Relationships

Spouse: Michael Joseph Copley (married 1933; Michael died Sep 17 1988)
Children (G25):

Stephen Michael Copley (b. Apr 29, 1936, Champaign IL)
Thomas Partlow Copley (b. Oct 29, 1944, Philadelphia PA)



Notes

Thomas Partlow Copley’s middle name “Partlow” honors her maiden name.
After Michael’s retirement in 1968, Tom’s family moved back to Berkeley to help care for their parents after Marion’s death.

People/Michael-Copley-Sr.title: Michael Copley Sr.
People/Michael-Copley-Sr.content:
Michael Copley Sr. (1813–1897)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Disambiguation

Not to be confused with Michael Joseph Copley (1898–1988), his grandson.
Not to be confused with Michael Copley (b. 1959), his great-great-grandson in the Stephen branch.

Biographical Profile
Michael Copley is the documented immigrant patriarch of the Copley family branch that settled in Lewis County, West Virginia. Family history and grave-marker evidence identify him as born 29 Sep 1813 in the parish of Kilgefin, County Roscommon, Ireland, and deceased in Lewis County, West Virginia in January 1897 (gravestone: 19 Jan 1897; narrative text includes 18 Jan 1897, a minor discrepancy to track).
He emigrated in the late 1830s and is strongly associated with the passenger record of Michael “Copely” arriving in New York on the Powhatan in August 1838. Family narrative and contextual evidence indicate an early labor phase connected to the Baltimore & Ohio Railroad (B&O) corridor, especially around Baltimore, Maryland and Potomac-route communities, followed by transition to settled agriculture.
In 1843, Michael and his brother Patrick Copley entered a purchase agreement for a 200-acre tract from Weeden Hoffman in what became the Cove Lick area near Weston. This transaction marks the family’s permanent establishment in the region.
Family Relationships

Spouse: Ann Copley (family tradition/secondary narratives identify her as Ann Elizabeth Munday)
Children (G24):

Mary Copley Quinn
John Copley
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley
Thomas “Tom” Copley
Sarah Copley


Siblings (Ireland/U.S. migration context): Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon, William Copley

Timeline Anchors

1813-09-29: Born, Kilgefin parish, Roscommon (per gravestone tradition).
1838-08 (manifest date 1838-08-20): Probable New York arrival on Powhatan as “Michael Copely.”
1838-1843 (inferred): Probable B&O labor period along Potomac corridor.
1843: 200-acre agreement with Weeden Hoffman, Lewis County.
1897-01: Death in Lewis County, WV.

Related Topic Pages

Irish Famine and Emigration
B&O Railroad Labor History
Irish Immigration to West Virginia

Research Gaps

Parents in Kilgefin (Q1): No direct parish record chain yet linking Michael to named parents.
Marriage record (Q3): Date/place for marriage to Ann remains unlocated.
Naturalization (Q19): Date, court, and certificate details unknown.
B&O service detail (Q16/Q17): Specific payroll/crew records not yet located.
Death-date discrepancy: 18 Jan vs 19 Jan 1897 across derivative narratives.

Acquisition Strategy

Irish parish registers (NLI): Systematic baptism search c.1808-1820 in Kilgefin and nearby Roscommon parishes for Michael + siblings.
Naturalization search by corridor: County/state courts in MD/VA/WV aligned to B&O routes; extract occupation and arrival data.
Marriage targeting: Catholic sacramental records and civil records in Potomac-adjacent communities (Harpers Ferry–Cumberland corridor).
Railroad records: Investigate B&O archival holdings and local historical collections for labor/contractor references.
Death reconciliation: Compare grave transcription, cemetery registers, and any obituary/church burial record.

Source Citations

COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf (uploaded source; pp. 3-17 for immigrant narrative, gravestone text, 1843 land agreement context).
copley_research_analysis (/home/ubuntu/copley_research_analysis.md) — synthesis of Q1, Q3, Q19, migration and settlement findings.
copley_research_findings (/home/ubuntu/copley_research_findings.md) — reliability and research strategy framework.
Powhatan manifest reference via findings synthesis (NARA microfilm pathway).
B&O historical context: www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/830

📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Robert “Bobby” Anthony Cox is a G27 descendant in the Stephen Michael Copley line — the son of Sara Copley Cox and Robert (Bob) Austin Cox.

Full name: Robert Anthony Cox
Birth: October 3, 1988, Sacramento, California
Parents: Sara Copley Cox and Robert (Bob) Austin Cox
Current location: Granite Bay, California (lives in his mother’s house; Sara is now itinerant for work)
Occupation: Owner, landscaping business
Partner: Nicole Brune (dating since July 2022)

Biography
Bobby was born in Sacramento, CA shortly after his parents moved from Southern California to the Sacramento area. His parents divorced in 1996 when he was seven, and he split time between his mother in Granite Bay and his father (and stepmother Diana) in Rancho Cordova. His early years were marked by school changes and social difficulty, but he found his footing in high school through friendships and weightlifting.
After high school he worked various jobs (Ace Hardware, Cost Plus World Market, Farmer’s Rice Co., landscaping) before dedicating himself to building his own landscaping business, which he pursued alongside academic coursework in horticulture and other subjects. He is described in the family history as deeply grateful to his family and committed to a mindful, purposeful lifestyle.

Father: Robert Austin Cox (hails from Torrance, CA; advertising career)
Stepmother: Diana (originally from Brea, CA; married Bob 1998; died October 2019)

People/Stephen-Joseph-Copley.content:
Stephen Joseph Copley (1970–2023)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Stephen Joseph Copley was born May 1, 1970 at Yale-New Haven Hospital, New Haven, Connecticut — the seventh and youngest child of Stephen Michael Copley and Marcia Thornton Copley. Shortly after his birth, the family relocated to Southern California when his father accepted a position at USC.
He was diagnosed early in life as autistic and severely developmentally disabled. As he grew older he required constant attention, was nonverbal, and prone to wandering. His care profoundly shaped the entire Copley family and directly influenced the careers and life paths of several of his siblings.

Birth: May 1, 1970, Yale-New Haven Hospital, New Haven, Connecticut
Death: June 10, 2023 (in his sleep, age 53)
Burial: San Juan Bautista, CA, August 8, 2023
Residence: Placed as a client of Harbor Regional Center; cared for at the Intercommunity Exceptional Children’s Home, Long Beach, CA; returned home on weekends
Legal status: Became a full-time ward of the State of California (at age 13, when parents divorced in 1983)

His father, Stephen Michael Copley, wrote his biographical sketch in the family history. A Celebration of Life ceremony was held September 7, 2023, at Skylinks Golf Course, Long Beach, CA.

“Stephen taught us all a new meaning of love. Through Stephen, we met other young children, individuals with disabilities and their parents, who otherwise we never would have known.” — Stephen Michael Copley

Family Relationships

Parents: Stephen Michael Copley, Marcia Thornton Copley
Siblings (G26): Michael Copley (b. 1959), Sara Copley Cox, Philip Copley, Paul Copley, Peter Copley, Susan Copley

Spouse: none; children: none

Phase-1-Questions-and-Answers.title: Phase 1 Questions and Answers
Phase-1-Questions-and-Answers.content:
Phase 1 Questions and Answers
This page addresses all Phase 1 quick-win questions from the strategy analysis and documents what is known, unknown, and the next action.
📊 Use Family Tree as the visual reference when mapping each research question to specific people and branches.
Q23 — Who was the “Tom” at Chancellorsville?
Current answer
Family tradition references a “Tom” who fought at Chancellorsville, but this appears to be an identity mismatch in the current compiled narrative. No reliable documentary chain yet links the story to a clearly identified person in this line (including Thomas E. Copley).
Gap analysis

Known: A family memory exists; narrative inconsistency has been flagged.
Unknown: Which Thomas (if any) served; exact relationship to core line.

Strategy to close gap

Build collateral-line matrix of all Thomas/Tom Copleys born c.1820–1850.
Search Civil War CMSR/pension indexes and unit rosters.
Reconcile with family tree timelines to eliminate impossible candidates.

Citations

Internal analysis note (Q23): file-based synthesis from family records.
NARA military records gateway: www.archives.gov/research/military/civil-war


Q45 — What is Nelle’s correct birth year (1896 vs 1897)?
Current answer
Working conclusion is 1897 (often listed as Dec 23, 1897), but conflicting references in family materials require direct primary verification.
Gap analysis

Known: Both 1896 and 1897 appear in compiled family text.
Unknown: Definitive civil/church record date.

Strategy to close gap

Retrieve Lewis County birth register entry and/or baptismal register.
Cross-check with earliest census age reporting and Social Security-era documents.

Citations

FamilySearch profile context for descendants: ancestors.familysearch.org/en/MVDR-37G/michael-joseph-copley-1898-1988
Lewis County genealogy research guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy


Q28 — Did John Copley serve in the Union/Quartermaster system?
Current answer
No conclusive proof yet. Existing web hints are weak and do not meet proof standards.
Gap analysis

Known: Family claim exists.
Unknown: Service unit, service dates, rank, pension trail.

Strategy to close gap

Request Compiled Military Service Records and pension records from NARA.
Specifically target Quartermaster Department records (Record Group 92).
Verify identity through county, age, and dependent records.

Citations

NARA Civil War records: www.archives.gov/research/military/civil-war
NARA military service requests: www.archives.gov/veterans/military-service-records


Q20 — Marriage date/place for John Copley and Mary Ellen Dolan Copley
Current answer
Marriage is inferred from child chronology and family records, but no definitive marriage certificate/parish entry has been located.
Gap analysis

Known: Couple identity and descendants are well-supported.
Unknown: Exact ceremony date, parish/county, officiant/witnesses.

Strategy to close gap

Search Lewis and Marshall County marriage books (c. 1880–1890).
Search Catholic sacramental registers in likely diocesan repositories.
Check newspaper marriage notices for both surnames.

Citations

Lewis County genealogy guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy


Q22 — Reconstruct the Dolan Family (Mary Ellen’s siblings)
Current answer
Parents are identified as Michael and Elizabeth Dolan in compiled materials, but sibling-level documentation remains sparse.
Gap analysis

Known: Core parental names and Moundsville connection are repeatedly cited.
Unknown: Full sibling list, migration pattern, occupations, parish ties.

Strategy to close gap

Build household reconstructions from census decades 1860–1910.
Search Marshall County probate/deed/church records for Dolan witnesses and heirs.
Use cluster research (neighbors/sponsors/godparents) to separate same-name individuals.

Citations

WV state and county archival leads via FamilySearch wiki: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy
Dolan surname resource (secondary): www.wikitree.com/genealogy/DOLAN


Q19 — Naturalization date/place for Michael Copley
Current answer
No naturalization document has been found yet; likely jurisdictions include early B&O corridor counties in MD/VA/WV.
Gap analysis

Known: Immigration period c. 1837–1838 is strongly supported by manifests.
Unknown: Court, petition date, witnesses, declaration/final papers.

Strategy to close gap

Search county-level naturalization indexes along rail corridor.
Correlate census citizenship columns with county court records.
Review NARA regional holdings and state archives.

Citations

NARA immigration/naturalization research: www.archives.gov/research/immigration
Passenger-list context (Powhatan/Kutusoff synthesis in findings report).


Q6 — What happened to William Copley in Australia?
Current answer
A plausible family hypothesis links William to anti-tithe unrest and transportation, but there is currently no direct documentary confirmation.
Gap analysis

Known: Story aligns with historical environment of the Irish Tithe War.
Unknown: Court file, ship transport record, colonial assignment, later life.

Strategy to close gap

Search Australian convict indexes and transport registers for surname variants.
Search Irish assize/petty sessions records c.1830–1840.
Link by age/place/family associates to avoid false positives.

Citations

Trove (National Library of Australia): trove.nla.gov.au
Convict Records (Australia): convictrecords.com.au


Q33–Q35 — Missing living-family biographical sketches
Current answer
Biographies for some modern descendants are uneven in depth. Core lines are known, but editorial completeness varies.
Gap analysis

Known: Major branches and several professional trajectories are documented.
Unknown: Standardized biographical summaries, source-backed timelines, and oral-history annotations for each living branch.

Strategy to close gap

Use a structured interview template for willing family members.
Collect publication/professional profile links and archive with permissions.
Tag oral-history claims separately from record-backed claims.

Citations

Method guidance from compiled analysis and findings documents.


Linked Research Entities

Places and topics: Kilgefin, Roscommon, Lewis County, West Virginia, B&O Railroad Labor, Copley No. 1 Oil Strike

Places/Baltimore-Maryland.title: Baltimore Maryland
Places/Baltimore-Maryland.content:
Baltimore, Maryland
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Coordinates: 39.2904, -76.6122
Current administration: Independent City of Baltimore, Maryland, USA.
Historical names/boundaries: “Baltimore City” and Archdiocese-based parish references are common in 19th-century records.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Baltimore was a major immigrant port and the strategic hub of the B&O Railroad system. Irish migrants moving into rail labor and western settlement corridors commonly passed through or worked from Baltimore-linked networks.

Economy: port commerce, rail administration, manufacturing.
Immigration pattern: strong Irish migration and labor recruitment base.
Record significance: rich Catholic and civil archival systems.

Copley Family Connection

Key context point for B&O Railroad Labor History and inferred early labor phase of Michael Copley Sr.
Important migration node for Irish Famine and Emigration.
High-priority location for locating potential marriage records for Michael Copley Sr and Ann Copley.
Also part of broader Maryland branch context for descendants like Ellen Bernadine Nelle Copley Sardo.

Related Topic Pages

B&O Railroad Labor History
Irish Famine and Emigration
Irish Immigration to West Virginia

Research Resources

Archdiocese of Baltimore Archives (parish marriages/baptisms).

Search 1838-1843 Catholic marriages for Copley/Munday variants.
Query B&O worker/contractor references in archival indexes.
Build Baltimore-to-Cumberland corridor chronology tied to family events.

Places/County-Roscommon-Ireland.content:
County Roscommon, Ireland
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Approx. center coordinates: 53.76, -8.27
Current administration: County Roscommon, Connacht, Republic of Ireland.
Historical names/boundaries: records distributed across parish, barony, and estate systems.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Roscommon was a core agrarian county with high tenancy pressure and persistent emigration. For genealogy, county-level context is essential because families could appear across neighboring parishes and spellings.

Economy: agriculture/tenancy with limited industrial absorption.
Migration pattern: sustained movement to U.S. labor and settlement fronts.
Research implication: triangulate church, land, tax, and estate records.

Copley Family Connection

Regional context for the pre-emigration generation tied to Michael Copley Sr.
Supports parish-level work in Kilgefin.
Helps frame unresolved parentage and sibling reconstruction questions.

Related Topic Pages

Irish Famine and Emigration
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Genealogical Research Methods

Research Resources

Roscommon local studies/library collections.
NLI registers: registers.nli.ie
Tithe books (Roscommon): titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie/pagestab/Roscommon/
Griffith’s valuation: www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/

Acquisition Strategy

Build county-wide surname occurrence table first, then narrow.
Compare adjacent-parish records for indirect kinship evidence.
Add estate-paper evidence where parish registers fail.

Places/Cove-Lick-West-Virginia.title: Cove Lick West Virginia
Places/Cove-Lick-West-Virginia.content:
Cove Lick, West Virginia
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Approx. coordinates (Copley Road/Cove Lick area): 39.05, -80.49
Current administration: Rural locality near Weston, Lewis County, West Virginia.
Historical names/boundaries: appears in road/tract descriptions more than as a formal town name.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Cove Lick represents land-based settlement geography: roads, tract lines, and deed descriptions rather than municipal addresses. For immigrant farm families, this was the practical center of household continuity.

Economy: farm production with later proximity to oil-era activity.
Record style: deeds, plats, metes-and-bounds descriptions.
Significance: transition from wage labor to rooted land tenure.

Copley Family Connection

Directly tied to Michael Copley Sr and Patrick Copley through the reported 1843 200-acre agreement with Weeden Hoffman.
Near-origin point for later continuity through John Copley and descendants.
Geographic backdrop to 1900 Copley Oil Strike lease and land-history questions.

Research Resources

Lewis County Clerk deed and plat books.
Historic topo/cadastral maps for Copley Road and adjacent tracts.
Local historical society collections and WV geological records.

Acquisition Strategy

Obtain certified copy of the 1843 transaction instrument.
Georeference parcel descriptions to modern map layers.
Correlate parcel transfers with probate and oil-lease records.

Source Notes

COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf

Current administration: Town of Jane Lew, Lewis County, West Virginia.
Historical names/boundaries: many 19th-century records use district/road descriptors rather than the town name.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Jane Lew is a useful Lewis County micro-region for interpreting settlement and movement around the Weston area. Even where direct references are sparse, it helps contextualize nearby census and deed localities.

Economy: mixed agriculture and local-route trade.
Research utility: geographic triangulation for “near Weston” family entries.
Evidence note: direct Copley-specific Jane Lew records remain limited in current corpus.

Copley Family Connection

Context page for county-locality interpretation tied to Michael Copley Sr, Ann Copley, and John Copley.
Supports locality disambiguation while mapping Lewis County residences.

Local cemetery/church transcriptions and district maps.
Lewis County historical publications and GIS overlays.

Places/Kilgefin-Ireland.title: Kilgefin Ireland
Places/Kilgefin-Ireland.content:
Kilgefin, Ireland
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Approx. coordinates: 53.73, -8.17 (parish-level approximation)
Current administration: County Roscommon, Connacht, Republic of Ireland.
Historical names/boundaries: Kilgefin/Kilgeffan spellings appear in older ecclesiastical records.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Kilgefin sat in a rural tenant-farming economy shaped by tithe pressure, insecure tenure, and heavy emigration. In this period, Roscommon families often left through chain migration before and after the Famine.

Economy: subsistence agriculture and tenancy.
Migration pattern: high out-migration to U.S. labor corridors.
Record challenge: fragmented Catholic registers.

Copley Family Connection

Reported birthplace of Michael Copley Sr (1813).
Sibling-origin context for Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon, William Copley.
Foundation point of the immigrant branch later established in West Virginia.

Research Resources

National Library of Ireland registers: registers.nli.ie
Roscommon Tithe Applotment Books: titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie/pagestab/Roscommon/
Griffith’s Valuation: www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/
Strokestown Park / estate-paper pathways for tenant lists.

Acquisition Strategy

Run surname-variant parish sweeps (Copley/Copely/Copeley).
Correlate tithe + valuation data with sibling-age hypotheses.
Add local-archivist or professional genealogist lookups for parish gaps.

Places/Kinawley-Ireland.title: Kinawley Ireland
Places/Kinawley-Ireland.content:
Kinawley, Ireland
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Approx. coordinates: 54.26, -7.45
Current administration: County Fermanagh area (Northern Ireland), near County Cavan boundary.
Historical names/boundaries: records can appear under Kinawley civil/parish forms with cross-border administrative complexity.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Kinawley was part of a rural borderland economy with heavy dependence on small farming. Family movement from this area into transatlantic migration streams was common in the early 19th century.

Economy: agricultural and parish-centered community life.
Migration pattern: family-based emigration to U.S. river/rail corridors.
Record challenge: scattered Catholic and civil archive pathways across jurisdictions.

Copley Family Connection

Reported birthplace of Ann Copley (Ann Elizabeth Munday tradition), born 1823.
Associated with family tradition that Ann’s father died in the Potomac after migration.
Critical origin node for verifying the Munday line.

Research Resources

PRONI (Public Record Office of Northern Ireland): parish/civil holdings.
NLI registers portal (cross-checking Catholic records): registers.nli.ie
Local Fermanagh genealogy societies and parish transcription projects.
Newspaper databases for surname variants tied to migration narratives.

Acquisition Strategy

Build Kinawley Munday/Monday surname matrix (1820-1850).
Cross-match with Potomac-corridor census clusters in U.S. records.
Prioritize baptisms/marriages that identify parent names and witnesses.

Places/Lewis-County-West-Virginia.content:
Lewis County, West Virginia
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Approx. center coordinates: 38.99, -80.45
Current administration: Lewis County, West Virginia, USA.
Historical names/boundaries: part of Virginia until 1863; county formed in 1816 from Harrison County.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Lewis County combined frontier agriculture with later oil-era economic shifts. It became the Copley family’s core settlement zone after immigration and labor-corridor movement.

Economy: farming, timber, and later oil impacts.
Institutional relevance: courthouse records in Weston are central to this family’s evidence trail.
Migration significance: transition from labor mobility to land tenure.

Copley Family Connection

Long-term residence for Michael Copley Sr and Ann Copley.
1843 land transaction with Weeden Hoffman anchors settlement chronology.
Multigenerational continuity through John Copley and descendants.
Central to 1900 Copley Oil Strike context.
Core destination in Irish Immigration to West Virginia.

Related Topic Pages

1900 Copley Oil Strike
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Genealogical Research Methods

Research Resources

Lewis County Clerk (Weston): deeds, probate, marriage records.
FamilySearch county guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy
WV Archives and History / Vital Records indexes.
County historical societies and local map collections.

Acquisition Strategy

Reconstruct full deed chain from 1843 forward.
Pull probate packets for 1897/1909/1925 milestones.
Join land records to census/tax rolls for household continuity.

Places/Weston-West-Virginia.content:
Weston, West Virginia
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Coordinates: 39.0418, -80.4673
Current administration: City of Weston, Lewis County seat, West Virginia.
Historical names/boundaries: records may appear under Lewis County, Virginia (pre-1863) and later West Virginia.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
As county seat, Weston concentrated legal, church, and market functions for surrounding farm communities. This made it a key records hub for marriage, probate, and land evidence.

Economy: county-seat services + surrounding agricultural production.
Administrative role: deed/probate/marriage archive center.
Church context: Catholic records in/near Weston are significant to Copley research.

Copley Family Connection

Birth-area context for John Copley (1843, near Weston).
Core record locus for Michael Copley Sr and Ann Copley family events.
Strong connection to farm continuity and oil-era legal documentation.

Research Resources

Lewis County Courthouse (Weston).
Parish archives for St. Bernard’s/Loveberry Ridge record pathways.
WV regional history repositories and Weston newspaper archives.

Acquisition Strategy

Extract marriage/probate records tied to John + Mary Ellen and siblings.
Build a Weston-linked household timeline from censuses/directories.
Reconcile courthouse and church date discrepancies.

The-Copley-Family-Narrative.title: The Copley Family Narrative
The-Copley-Family-Narrative.content:
The Copley Family Narrative
📊 For a generation-by-generation visual guide to the people in this story, see Family Tree.
Chapter 1 — Irish Roots
The wind moved low and cold over the fields of Kilgefin, Roscommon, and the Copley children learned early that land could be both cradle and trial. In this parish world of chapel bells and tenancy pressure, Michael Copley came of age with siblings who would become part of the family’s migration story: Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon, and William Copley.
The records are thin, but the pressure is visible in the era itself—tithe disputes, agrarian strain, and uncertainty for Catholic families in the 1820s and 1830s.1
[Speculative based on typical patterns in western Irish migration: Michael likely grew up in a kin-and-neighbor network where departure was first discussed as a temporary labor strategy, not a permanent goodbye.]
And in nearby Fermanagh, Ann Elizabeth Munday Copley began her own path. Family memory says her father died in the Potomac far from home—a sorrow that traveled ahead of her into the American story.2
Chapter 2 — The Journey
By the late 1830s, the Atlantic had become a bridge for survival. Passenger lists suggest the siblings did not all travel together: Patrick and Bridget likely sailed first, with Michael following as “Copely” on a later manifest.3
The ships reached New York, but New York was not the destination. Work was. Rail grades, canals, roads, and rough camps pulled immigrants inland. The Copley path, like so many Irish paths, appears to have moved toward the labor frontier where wages, however harsh, could be turned into land.
[Speculative based on labor patterns: their first American years probably alternated between temporary boarding, camp work, and chain-migration support from already-arrived siblings.]
Chapter 3 — Railroad Days
In the shadow of expanding track and timbered hills, the family’s center of gravity shifted to Lewis County, West Virginia (then Virginia). The 1843 agreement with Weeden Hoffman—200 acres tied to long-term payment—was not just a contract; it was a declaration of intention.4
If rail labor gave them entry, land gave them permanence. The Copleys built around field seasons, church routines, and hard winters. Michael Copley and Ann Elizabeth Munday Copley raised a large family, and one son, John Copley, would hold the line into the next century.
The oral tradition of camp labor and settlement along the B&O corridor fits the broader history of Irish workers in the region, even as the payroll proof for Michael remains elusive.5
Chapter 4 — Oil and Opportunity
In September 1900, the story cracked open with the earth itself. The Copley No. 1 Oil Strike came in with enormous force, reportedly around 4,800 barrels per day. In local memory it was spectacle—black flow, overflow into creek beds, and suddenly, new possibilities.6
For John Copley and Mary Ellen Dolan Copley, this was opportunity tempered by loss. Mary Ellen died in 1901, close behind the strike, and the family had to navigate grief while managing a transformed asset base.
[Speculative pending lease/probate confirmation: the oil revenues likely financed education choices for the children in ways impossible on farm income alone.]
Chapter 5 — New Generations
The children of John and Mary Ellen carried the family into professional America. Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo moved into nursing and education; Michael Joseph Copley moved into advanced chemistry and federal scientific leadership. Their lines spread through medicine, engineering, academia, business, social work, and policy.
From Roscommon fields to laboratories and lecture halls, the family arc widened across states and decades. Yet the old questions remain at the edge of the page: Who were Michael’s parents in Kilgefin? Where is the record of Michael and Ann’s marriage? What documents settle the Quartermaster claim, the Dolan reconstruction, the oil royalty story?
So the narrative closes where good genealogy always opens—at the archive door.

B&O Railroad Labor History
Irish immigrants in the 1840s–1850s were heavily recruited into difficult rail construction work, including westward B&O expansion through the Mid-Atlantic and what became West Virginia. This gives strong contextual support to the claim that Michael Copley worked in rail labor before permanent settlement.

Historical context: infrastructure boom, dangerous manual labor, immigrant labor camps.
What is sourced: broad pattern is well documented in regional history.
What remains open: specific payroll/personnel proof for Michael.

Sources:

e-WV Irish in West Virginia: www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/830
West Virginia and the Irish (context article): wvmetronews.com/2024/03/18/west-virginia-and-the-irish/


1900 Copley Oil Strike
In September 1900, South Penn Oil’s Copley No. 1 well on family land reportedly flowed at roughly 4,800 barrels/day, becoming a landmark event in West Virginia petroleum history and a turning point in family wealth.

Historical context: early 20th-century Appalachian petroleum expansion.
What is sourced: marker history, period oil publications, local historical accounts.
What remains open: exact lease terms, lifetime royalties, and heir distribution (Q24–Q26).

Sources:

HMDB marker context: www.hmdb.org/results.asp%20Virginia&County=Lewis%20County
The Oil Well Driller (1905): archive.org/stream/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich_djvu.txt


Irish Origins in Kilgefin, Ireland
The strongest family-origin anchor is Michael’s gravestone claim of birth in Kilgefin parish, Roscommon. This places the lineage in a region where parish records, tithe books, and valuation materials are key to pre-Famine reconstruction.

Historical context: Catholic households under tithe pressure and land insecurity.
What is sourced: place-origin claim and available record systems.
What remains open: parentage proof for Michael Copley (Q1), confirmation of sibling cluster in parish records.

Sources:

National Library of Ireland registers portal: registers.nli.ie
Tithe Applotment portal (Roscommon): titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie/pagestab/Roscommon/
Griffith’s Valuation portal: www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/


Immigration to West Virginia via New York and the Potomac Corridor
Detailed page: Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Passenger records suggest staggered arrivals: Patrick/Bridget (1837, Kutusoff) and Michael (1838, Powhatan as “Copely”). Subsequent movement likely followed labor corridors toward rail and settlement zones.

Historical context: chain migration and labor-following settlement.
What is sourced: manifests and local settlement chronology.
What remains open: arrival route for Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon (Q8/Q10).

Sources:

NARA immigration research hub: www.archives.gov/research/immigration
Lewis County genealogy context: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy


Lewis County, West Virginia Settlement and Land Tenure
The 1843 agreement with Weeden Hoffman marks the family’s shift from mobile labor to rooted landholding. Long-horizon payment and eventual title completion mirror frontier-capital constraints of immigrant households.

Historical context: installment acquisition, speculation-era title chains.
What is sourced: agreement existence and locale.
What remains open: full courthouse title chain and deed record alignment.

Sources:

County history listing (book market references):

www.amazon.com/Lewis-County-West-Virginia-crossroads/dp/0898658675
www.abebooks.com/Lewis-County-West-Virginia-pictorial-history/32167392339/bd




From Frontier Farming to Professional Careers
Across generations, the family transitions from immigrant labor and agrarian life to medicine, nursing, chemistry, engineering, and academic leadership. Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo and Michael Joseph Copley are central bridge figures.

Historical context: education as mobility engine in 20th-century America.
What is sourced: educational/professional identities in family reports and public profiles.
What remains open: quantitative link between oil income and educational spending.

Sources:

FamilySearch profile context for Michael Joseph Copley: ancestors.familysearch.org/en/MVDR-37G/michael-joseph-copley-1898-1988


Cross-Theme Linkage
These themes are interdependent:

Kilgefin, Ireland origin uncertainty drives Q1.
B&O Railroad Labor History context explains migration-to-settlement mechanics.
1900 Copley Oil Strike likely underwrites transition to advanced professions.
Academic and Scientific Achievement documents professional outcomes.
Unresolved documentary points (marriages, naturalization, service records) define the next archival phase.

Topics/1900-Copley-Oil-Strike.title: 1900 Copley Oil Strike
Topics/1900-Copley-Oil-Strike.content:
1900 Copley Oil Strike
Historical Context
The Copley No. 1 well (South Penn Oil) was drilled on Copley-associated land in Lewis County, West Virginia and reportedly came in during September 1900 at roughly 4,800 barrels/day. Within Appalachian petroleum history, this is treated as a major local strike and a turning point in the county’s oil economy.
Broader forces shaping the event:

Rapid late-19th/early-20th century expansion of Appalachian oil leasing and drilling.
Increasing value of private mineral rights in agrarian counties.
Transition from household farming economies to royalty-linked cash flow.

Copley Family Connection

Core family figures tied to the event context:

John Copley
Michael Copley Sr
Ann Copley
Michael Joseph Copley


Key location anchors:

Lewis County, West Virginia
 Copley Road area
Weston, West Virginia



Family tradition and synthesis documents consistently frame the strike as the major economic inflection point that helped support later educational and professional advancement in the family’s 20th-century branches.
Timeline

1843: Hoffman-Copley land agreement establishes long-term Copley landholding base.
c. 1899-1900: Leasing/drilling phase by South Penn Oil on/near Copley lands.
1900-09: Copley No. 1 reportedly flows at ~4,800 bpd.
1900s onward: Royalty/lease value becomes a major research question in family economic history.
1925: Death of John Copley; probate period is a key target for inheritance distribution evidence.

Primary Sources

Historical Marker Database (Copley No. 1 context):

www.hmdb.org/results.asp%20Virginia&County=Lewis%20County

archive.org/stream/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich/oilwelldrillerhi00whitrich_djvu.txt

www.abebooks.com/Lewis-County-West-Virginia-pictorial-history/32167392339/bd

Research Gaps

Exact lease terms (bonus, royalty percentage, duration, assignments).
Verified lifetime production and total revenue of Copley No. 1.
Precise distribution of oil-derived wealth among heirs.
Whether multiple adjacent Copley-linked parcels were leased/drilled.

Acquisition Strategies

Pull deed + lease books around 1899-1901; extract all Copley/South Penn entries.
Build a royalty ledger from probate, tax, and deed references.
Cross-check production claims against period trade publications and state reports.

Academic and Scientific Achievement
Historical Context
A major long-term theme in Copley history is intergenerational upward mobility from immigrant labor/farming roots into advanced education, research, and professional leadership. This shift mirrors wider U.S. 20th-century trends in higher education expansion, technical professions, and federal research institutions.
Copley Family Connection

Michael Joseph Copley — chemist; family materials describe major USDA laboratory leadership.
Stephen Michael Copley — physics/materials-science trajectory with academic and industrial leadership roles.
Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo — nursing/education branch bridge.

Topics/B-and-O-Railroad-Labor-History.content:
B&O Railroad Labor History
Historical Context
In the 1840s-1850s, the Baltimore & Ohio Railroad expansion relied heavily on immigrant labor, especially Irish workers. Conditions were typically harsh: dangerous grading/tunneling work, unstable camp conditions, and irregular pay structures through contractor systems.
This labor history is central to understanding how many newly arrived Irish families moved from port entry into inland settlement.
Copley Family Connection

Individuals directly connected by narrative/context:

Michael Copley Sr
Ann Copley
Patrick Copley


Places tied to labor corridor reconstruction:

Baltimore, Maryland
Lewis County, West Virginia
Weston, West Virginia



Current evidence level is high for historical pattern, moderate/low for named payroll proof for Michael specifically.
Timeline

1828: B&O Railroad founded; progressive westward buildout follows.
1830s-1840s: Irish immigrant labor concentration rises along B&O projects.
Late 1830s-early 1840s (inferred): Probable labor phase for Michael Copley Sr after U.S. arrival.
1843: Copley land agreement in Lewis County marks shift from mobile labor to settled farming.
Later 19th century: Rail-connected markets reinforce county economic integration.

Primary Sources

www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/830


WV MetroNews context essay:

wvmetronews.com/2024/03/18/west-virginia-and-the-irish/

B&O Railroad Museum Library & Archives (see local catalog/collection guides)

Personnel/contractor indexes in B&O collections.
Naturalization petitions in counties along B&O corridor.

Research Gaps

No firm start/end dates for Copley-linked railroad labor period.

Topics/Genealogical-Research-Methods.title: Genealogical Research Methods
Topics/Genealogical-Research-Methods.content:
Genealogical Research Methods
Historical Context
Copley research spans multiple jurisdictions (Ireland, U.S. federal records, West Virginia county repositories, Catholic parish systems) and mixed evidence quality (primary records, compiled narratives, oral history). A method-first approach is required to avoid conflation, especially with repeated names and variant spellings.
Copley Family Connection
Method priorities in this project focus on:

Irish parentage reconstruction for Michael Copley Sr and Ann Copley.
Immigration and labor-pathway documentation for early U.S. years.
Land, lease, and probate economics in Lewis County, West Virginia.
Professional-lineage documentation for Michael Joseph Copley and Stephen Michael Copley.

High-value linked hubs:

Bibliography and Acquisition Guide
1900 Copley Oil Strike
Irish Famine and Emigration

Timeline (Research Program)

Phase A: Assemble known family narrative and disambiguate repeated names.
Phase B: Build evidence matrix by question (Q1, Q3, Q8, Q16, Q24, etc.).
Phase C: Acquire targeted primary records (parish, manifests, deeds, probate).
Phase D: Integrate DNA and documentary findings for kinship confidence scoring.
Phase E: Publish revised narrative with citation tiers and unresolved-gap register.

Primary Sources
Core record systems and repositories:

NLI Catholic parish records: registers.nli.ie
Tithe Applotment Books (Roscommon): titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie/pagestab/Roscommon/
Griffith’s Valuation: www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/
NARA immigration resources: www.archives.gov/research/immigration
FamilySearch county genealogy guides and catalog.
Lewis County Clerk (deed/probate/marriage books in Weston).
Internal project docs:

Copley_Research_Strategy.pdf
copley_research_analysis
copley_research_findings



DNA Testing Strategies

Prioritize targeted descendant tests from distinct sibling branches.
Cluster matches by shared segments and triangulated ancestral couples.
Use documentary constraints to validate (not replace) DNA inferences.

Archive Research Techniques

Build a question-to-record-type matrix before searching.
Capture full citation metadata (repository, call number, image/page IDs).
Track negative searches to prevent duplicate effort.

Record Acquisition Methods

Courthouse pull list by date range and document class.
Parish register extraction templates for witnesses/sponsors.
Manifest surname-variant sweep (Copley/Copely/Cople/Coplee).

Research Gaps

Missing primary marriage record for Michael Copley Sr + Ann Copley.
No direct payroll proof yet for B&O labor service.
Incomplete economic reconstruction of oil-lease and inheritance patterns.
Need broader DNA coverage in collateral lines for robust kinship triangulation.

Acquisition Strategies

Sequence acquisition by highest-impact unresolved questions.
Use a confidence rubric (primary, secondary, oral) on every claim.
Reconcile contradictions (e.g., date conflicts) with explicit evidence logs.

Topics/Irish-Famine-and-Emigration.title: Irish Famine and Emigration
Topics/Irish-Famine-and-Emigration.content:
Irish Famine and Emigration
Historical Context
The Great Famine (1840s-1850s) was the most dramatic emigration driver in 19th-century Ireland, but outmigration pressures began earlier and continued long after the famine peak. Rural families in western counties, including Roscommon and nearby regions, faced tenancy insecurity, food vulnerability, and limited economic mobility.
For this project, famine-era context matters because it explains:

Why Irish households accepted high-risk migration pathways.
How chain migration and kin networks accelerated relocation.
Why labor corridors in U.S. port and rail regions became common first destinations.

Copley Family Connection

Family origin anchors tied to pre-/famine-era Ireland:

Michael Copley Sr (Kilgefin, Roscommon tradition)
Ann Copley (Kinawley/Fermanagh tradition)


Place links:

County Roscommon, Ireland
Kilgefin, Ireland
Kinawley, Ireland
Lewis County, West Virginia



Although the Copley arrival chronology begins in the late 1830s, famine-era pressures remain crucial for understanding continued migration of relatives, neighbors, and labor-network companions into the same American corridors.
Timeline

1820s-1830s: High tenancy pressure and agrarian instability in western Ireland.
1837-1838: Reported Copley-linked U.S. arrivals (Patrick/Bridget, then Michael as “Copely”).
1845-1852: Great Famine years intensify transatlantic migration from Irish counties.
1840s-1850s: Expansion of Irish labor corridors into Mid-Atlantic rail and settlement zones.
By mid/late 19th century: Established Copley household rooted in Lewis County.

www.archives.gov/research/immigration

Research Gaps

Parentage-level proof for Michael Copley Sr in Kilgefin parish records.
Definitive migration path and arrival details for Ann Copley.
Corroboration of sibling migration cluster timing and shared route.
Relationship between famine-era departures and known Copley-linked arrivals.

Acquisition Strategies

Map candidate households in Roscommon/Fermanagh to U.S. arrival cohorts.
Use cluster genealogy: reconstruct companions/witnesses in U.S. records.

Topics/Irish-Immigration-to-West-Virgini.title: Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Topics/Irish-Immigration-to-West-Virgini.content:
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Historical Context
Irish migration into what became West Virginia came in overlapping waves tied to transportation corridors, labor demand, and affordable land opportunities. Workers often entered through Atlantic ports, moved through rail/turnpike labor systems, and then transitioned into family farming and local trade.
This pattern provides the macro-framework for Copley migration and settlement in Lewis County.
Copley Family Connection

Core lineage links:

Michael Copley Sr
Ann Copley
John Copley


Primary place links:

County Roscommon, Ireland
Kilgefin, Ireland
Lewis County, West Virginia
Weston, West Virginia



The Copley pathway reflects a common immigrant sequence: origin locality → migration corridor labor → county-level landholding → multigenerational rooted community.
Timeline

1830s: Copley-linked arrivals documented/reported in U.S. passenger lists.
1840s: Irish labor flows into transportation corridors in Mid-Atlantic/Appalachian zones.
1843: Copley/Hoffman land agreement marks permanent settlement arc.
Late 1800s: Consolidation of family land and local social institution ties.
1900 onward: Oil-era inflection and later upward educational mobility.
